Yes, you are right. I think is a better solution because we don't have the necessity to define another setting in the config. If you want a different payload you only need to rewrite the method get_security_payload() in the User Model. 

So, in the view.py file we get the payload:

```python
response = dict()
if include_user:
    response['user'] = form.user.get_security_payload()
```

```python
class UserMixin(BaseUserMixin):
    # ...
    def get_security_payload(self):
        """Serialize user object as response payload."""
        return {'id': str(self.id)}
```

If you need a different Payload redifine the method: 

```python
UserModel(Base):
    id = db.Column(db.Integer, primary_key=True)
    email = db.Column(db.String(120), unique=True, nullable=False)
    password = db.Column(db.String(255))
    name = db.Column(db.String(80))
    
    def get_security_payload(self):
        return {
            id: self.id,
            name: self.name,
            email: self.email  
        }
```

Though if you simply do `pip install azure-cli` you likely will have to be as it attempts to install globally.

There are a couple of options if you are not an administrator:
1. User a virtual environment and install the CLI in there.
```
> virtualenv env
> env \Scripts\activate
> pip install azure-cli
```

2. Use pip to install in user directory
```
> pip install --user azure-cli
```

In that case we're providing a generic enabled / disabled flag that monkey patches the stdlib (for obvious reasons because probably there aren't so many different ways to enable that by default). While it's interesting to provide an automatic full ON / full OFF functionality, I think that it could be better and a bit more generic to provide a helper function that can be used to say: "let's propagate the Context only for this Task".

This means that the underlying `_create_task` may become a helper function like `run_in_executor` ([ref][1]) so that it's explicit when you're going to propagate the context to a new task:
```python
from ddtrace.contrib.asyncio import helpers

# propagates the context
helpers.create_task(coro())

# doesn't propagate the context
loop.create_task(coro())
```

The main reason is that in some cases we don't want the automatic propagation when creating new tasks (e.g. a very long-running Task that lasts 2+ minutes could break the stats for users requests). On the other hand, that helper should be enough to wrap easily the `BaseEventLoop.create_task`, if someone needs to enable that by default everywhere.

Is it reasonable for you if we move that function as a helper that must be used manually, so that you can later wrap your `loop.create_task` with this one in your code base?
If it is, we may think that your `_create_task` is the first building block that anyone can use to manually or automatically propagate the `Context`. Later on, we may think to keep it enabled by default but for the moment, maybe it's more generic to provide a helper and some patterns to enable it.

[1]: https://github.com/thehesiod/dd-trace-py/blob/b9a41ec6cd7f8636cdbddb2134eaab88604f085e/ddtrace/contrib/asyncio/helpers.py#L36

* Torch has 0.1: https://github.com/torch/nn/blob/eb13695/BatchNormalization.lua#L46

* MXNet has 0.9, which probably means 0.1: http://mxnet.readthedocs.org/en/latest/python/symbol.html#mxnet.symbol.BatchNorm

We can add a comment about this to the code.

If we're only using whole sentences, batches will almost never have the right number of words.  If our batch size is 4096, we can add sentences until either we exceed 4096 or the next sentence would exceed 4096.  If all of our sentences are length 100, we could choose a batch size of 4000 or 4100.

When we have multiple devices, we also want the number of sentences to be evenly divisible so we won't waste computation (some devices processing N examples per batch, some N+1).

The two most direct solutions are:
1. Set number of sentences to the multiple of number of devices that gets us closest to the target batch size.  (Num devices can influence batch size)
2. Set number of sentences to be as close as possible to the target batch size and add padding sentences to make the batch size divisible by number of devices.  (Wasted computation for majority of batches)

This implementation goes with option 1.

You weren't actually forwarded to ftp.m.o in the sense of a 302. The headers you get from a request to the CDN are sum of
* the request from CDN to origin server
* your request to the CDN
The origin server is a vhost on the same cluster that serves ftp.m.o, so you get this sort of thing
  X-Backend-Server: ftp5.dmz.scl3.mozilla.com
when making a CDN request, and it doesn't mean you talked to ftp.m.o directly.

The origin server for https://download-installer.cdn.mozilla.net blocks downloads of .mar files at the time of download, but doesn't modify directory listings. eg if you try to download
 https://download-installer.cdn.mozilla.net/pub/firefox/releases/36.0/update/win32/en-US/firefox-34.0.5-36.0.partial.mar
you get a 403.

@scottpurdy: there are a few things going on here that are not quite right. 

1. It's better to remove the SYSLOG_HANDLER altogether from this script and from nupic-logging.conf, because we actually don't make use of it due to critical limitations. At some point, we experimented with SysLogHandler on Linux and Mac OS X. We found that it didn't work  with long log lines that we often need for debugging, such as capturing a deep traceback. We experimented and found that the two platforms differed in how they handled "overflow": one of them would drop the log message, while the other would truncate at the limit. So, we decided against using them. We must have forgotten to remove the SYSLOG_HANDLER references from this script and from nupic-logging.conf. You can see that `nupic.support.initLogging()` doesn't actually add SysLogHandler to the `handlers` list, so it's not going to be used.
2. SysLogHandler is not supported on windows, but the above line recommends to get rid of this stuff, anyway. If we really need to support SysLogHandler and NTEventLogHandler-like functionality, then we would need an additional effort to make it work right. If there is no such requirement, let's just drop this SysLogHandler/NEventLogHandler functionality altogether.
 ```
  if platform.startswith('darwin'):
    replacements[makeKey('SYSLOG_HANDLER_ADDRESS')] = '"/var/run/syslog"'
  elif platform.startswith('linux'):
    replacements[makeKey('SYSLOG_HANDLER_ADDRESS')] = '"/dev/log"'
  elif platform.startswith('win'):
    replacements[makeKey('SYSLOG_HANDLER_ADDRESS')] = '"log"'
  else:
    raise RuntimeError("This platform is neither darwin, win32, nor linux: %s" % (
      sys.platform,))
```
3. The first block of code below was intended to initialize file logging in the suppressed state; hence, `/dev/null`. However, the `'win'` initialization doesn't match that intent and needs to be fixed. It's only in the `if configLogDir is not None:` block that we should be enabling file logging.
 ```
  # Nupic logs go to file
  replacements[makeKey('PERSISTENT_LOG_HANDLER')] = 'fileHandler'
  if platform.startswith('win'):
    replacements[makeKey('FILE_HANDLER_LOG_FILENAME')] = '"nupic.log"'
  else:
    replacements[makeKey('FILE_HANDLER_LOG_FILENAME')] = '"/dev/null"'

  # Set up log file path for the default file handler and configure handlers
  handlers = list()
  
  if configLogDir is not None:
    logFilePath = _genLoggingFilePath()
    makeDirectoryFromAbsolutePath(os.path.dirname(logFilePath))
    replacements[makeKey('FILE_HANDLER_LOG_FILENAME')] = repr(logFilePath)

    handlers.append(replacements[makeKey('PERSISTENT_LOG_HANDLER')])
```

Yes this is intentional. `schema` is descriptive attribute storing the schema for the service (see Cornices/cornice#376), and `colander_validator` is just a shorthand validator that validate the request against the schema defined on the service. The original intention was to to keep schema and validation two different concerns, and I think that swagger should use only the schema because it serves the same precise concern (describing the service).
The introduction of `colander_body_validator` broke that behaviour, schema and validator became tightly coupled, so `colander_body_validator` should be treated as special case.

I know that looks awkward, but that's a subject/verb agreement issue in English. Plural subjects, like "tests" (which terminology we inherit from the `setup` keyword) need a plural verb...which happens to be "require" (without an 's'), while singular subjects, like 'chameleon' need a singular verb, which happens to be "requires" (with the 's'). So "tests requires" would be as wrong as "chameleon  require".

Some nomenclature musings.  If we have "datecreated", "datemodified", and "earliestdate" dates, then I'm afraid things may not be fully clear what the latter means, based on its sole name.  Partially perhaps because one can say "earliest modification date" which is not the same as the creation date, or "earliest citation date" which would be the date of the first citation, or "earliest publication date" in case a work was re-published, or "earliest X date" in general.  Seems like the qualifier X is missing, as it were.

It may be worth pondering whether we shouldn't find better name.  And, perhaps, whether we actually *need* a new DB column for storing it?  Wouldn't it be sufficient to have the above BibCheck rule running periodically to force some designed new metadata field, say 260/269 or something, to be equal to the earliest publication date?  For example, do we want to expose this information to cataloguers, or is it just a "derived field" living in the database for convenience?  Are we getting more derived fields of this kind, such as "latest refextract date", "latest keywording date", as we mused elsewhere could be convenient for internal processing?

>  it does not assume any more that MySQL and Invenio run on the same system

FWIW we have been recommending to run Apache and MySQL on separate hosts since many, many years... The GRANT instructions were simply printed out and people were left to set them up themselves, using real IP addresses.  The "easy-db-init" way was used mostly on local developer boxes only.

As for the "opening up the barriers",  it is true that allowing `%` widens possibilities for an accidental break-in or a mishap, e.g. in case people use the same credentials locally and on a remote system.  However the DB is usually protected by another level, e.g. Invenio Puppet module updates DB OS firewall for each declared Web and Worker nodes, hence the situation should not be too bad.

Anyway, if the main motivation was to allow connectivity from Docker containers, one could in theory test whether the init command is run from within Docker, and allow `%` then and only then.  E.g. by checking `$(cat /proc/1/cgroup | grep -c docker)`.

(Another, even simpler possibility to address "opening up the barrier" concerns would be to document and print out the information that `%` was used, so that the admin would be informed first hand.)

This is backwards.  If `julian_date is None`, then this routine returns the [true ecliptic coordinates](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ecliptic_coordinate_system).  If you give it a date, then it gives you the coordinates with respect to the sun being at (0,0).

Also, python has a [datetime](https://docs.python.org/2/library/datetime.html) module.  Perhaps we ought to use that for passing around the date.  

[Here](http://code-highlights.blogspot.com/2013/01/julian-date-in-python.html) is code to convert from that to the julian date.  But I'm not sure how many people will find the raw julian date value very useful.
